title.en: Gym or Coaching Centre Refusing a Fee Refund? Your Consumer Rights

answerBox.en: Coaching centres and gyms taking lump-sum fees are service providers under consumer law — "no refund under any circumstances" clauses are unfair contract terms the 2019 Act lets commissions strike down. For coaching, UGC/regulator rules and the 2024 coaching guidelines back proportionate refunds on withdrawal. Demand in writing, escalate via 1915, then e-Daakhil — most fee disputes are small-value and cost nothing to file.

en.title: Date your exit and compute the proportion
en.summary: Refund maths: fees paid minus services actually availed.
en.detail: Withdraw **in writing** (email + WhatsApp) stating the last date of attendance/service and demand the **proportionate refund** — total paid minus a reasonable charge for the period actually used and genuine one-time costs. Consumer commissions consistently order proportionate refunds against lump-sum forfeitures; the Supreme Court and UGC have condemned full-fee forfeiture in education, and the **2024 central coaching-centre guidelines** prescribe pro-rata refunds on withdrawal.

en.title: "No refund" clauses don't end the matter
en.summary: Unfair contract terms are voidable under the 2019 Act.
en.detail: The Consumer Protection Act, 2019 expressly empowers commissions to declare **unfair contract terms** null — one-sided forfeitures, penalties disproportionate to loss, and terms letting one party alter unilaterally are the listed examples. A receipt stamped "fees once paid are non-refundable" is a negotiating posture, not the law. Misleading ads (topper photos, "guaranteed selection") add an unfair-trade-practice count.

en.title: Escalate: 1915, then the ₹999 notice
en.summary: Small operators fold at the notice; chains at the docket.
en.detail: Register on **[1915 / consumerhelpline.gov.in](https://consumerhelpline.gov.in)** — chains and ed-tech companies respond to NCH dockets. Then an **advocate's legal notice** demanding the computed refund with a 15-day deadline. For ed-tech/EMI-financed courses (loan-linked subscriptions), also write to the lending NBFC disputing further EMIs on the withdrawn service — RBI-regulated lenders take documented service-withdrawal disputes seriously.

en.title: File on e-Daakhil — small claims are the point
en.summary: Zero fee up to ₹5 lakh; claim refund + interest + costs.
en.detail: File on **[e-Daakhil](https://edaakhil.nic.in)** from your city (process in our [consumer forum guide](/help/consumer-complaints/consumer-forum-complaint-guide)): the fee receipt, brochure/ad screenshots, your withdrawal letter and the refusal are the whole evidence set. Claim the proportionate refund with interest, compensation for harassment and litigation costs. Gym closures/relocations mid-membership are straightforward deficiency — the unserved period is refundable as of right.

rows.label: e-Daakhil filing
rows.note: Typical fee disputes fit the zero slab

en.footnote: Enrolling? Pay term-wise rather than multi-year lump sums, and screenshot the promises (batch size, faculty, hours) — the ad is evidence when the service shrinks after payment.

en: coaching centre fees wapas kaise le

en.q: I signed a form saying fees are non-refundable. Am I bound?
en.a: Not absolutely. The 2019 Act lets commissions strike unfair contract terms, and disproportionate forfeiture of fees for unrendered services is the standard example. You remain liable for the period/services actually used — the rest is recoverable despite the clause.

en.q: How much refund can I realistically expect?
en.a: The proportionate figure: total fees minus the used period (and genuinely incurred one-time costs like registration/kit at reasonable value). Leaving a 12-month course after 2 months typically supports ~10/12ths recovery, plus interest when litigated. Commission orders follow exactly this arithmetic.

en.q: The coaching centre shut down/shifted far away. Full refund?
en.a: Yes for the unserved portion — closure or material relocation is deficiency in service; your remedy is refund of the balance with interest, and compensation where the disruption cost you (mid-session, exam year). File even if the operator claims "adjustment in another branch" you never agreed to.

en.q: My course was financed by an EMI/loan through the ed-tech. Do I keep paying?
en.a: Dispute in writing with both the platform and the NBFC immediately on withdrawal — continued EMIs for a cancelled service are contestable, and RBI-regulated lenders must process such disputes. Do not simply default silently (credit score); create the written dispute record, then escalate as a consumer against both.

en.q: Can I also complain about fake "toppers" advertising?
en.a: Yes — misleading advertisement is an unfair trade practice under the 2019 Act, with CCPA empowered to penalise institutes (and it has, repeatedly, in coaching). Add the ad screenshots to your complaint; it strengthens compensation and puts regulatory pressure behind your refund.

reviewer: Adv. Sneha Iyer, Bar Council of Tamil Nadu & Puducherry, TN/1187/2013
